title: Conveyor Belt Speed and Jam Detection
spl:
index=ot sourcetype="conveyor:sensor"
| eval jam=if(lower(jam_sensor)="true" OR jam_sensor="1",1,0)
| bin _time span=1m
| stats avg(speed_fpm) as avg_speed, avg(motor_amps) as avg_amps, max(jam) as jam_flag by line_id, belt_id, _time
| where avg_speed < 10 OR jam_flag=1 OR avg_amps>25
| table _time, line_id, belt_id, avg_speed, avg_amps, jam_flag
description: Belt slowdowns and jams starve downstream stations and can damage product; fast detection limits cascade stops across the line.
value: Service and risk leaders use signals from Conveyor Belt Speed and Jam Detection to move crews, budget, and customer or regulator communications while the situation is still controllable, so the organization steers from live data rather than a post-incident report.
implementation: Map photo-eye, encoder, and VFD feedback through OPC-UA into Edge Hub with 1-second sampling aggregated in Splunk. Set nominal speed per SKU from MES lookup if variable. Tie jam events to video timestamp if optional stream metadata is ingested. **Domain context:** Jam cascades are a common OEE loss; nominal speed and amp draw thresholds depend on belt load and SKU—tune `avg_speed`/`avg_amps` limits per line. **Splunk:** Aggregate before alert noise—1m `bin` as shown; lift raw sampling rate only for RCA, not for 24/7 alerting cost.
visualization: Line chart (speed and amps), Status timeline (jam), Table (active issues), Single value (lines stopped).
knownFalsePositives: Jam or speed variance on wet belts, new SKUs, or after mechanical adjustment during the maintenance window the line documented.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Edge Hub.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype="conveyor:sensor"` (line_id, belt_id, speed_fpm, motor_amps, jam_sensor).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Map photo-eye, encoder, and VFD feedback through OPC-UA into Edge Hub with 1-second sampling aggregated in Splunk. Set nominal speed per SKU from MES lookup if variable. Tie jam events to video timestamp if optional stream metadata is ingested. **Domain context:** Jam cascades are a common OEE loss; nominal speed and amp draw thresholds depend on belt load and SKU—tune `avg_speed`/`avg_amps` limits per line. **Splunk:** Aggregate before alert noise—1m `bin` as shown; lift raw sampling rate only …

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="conveyor:sensor"
| eval jam=if(lower(jam_sensor)="true" OR jam_sensor="1",1,0)
| bin _time span=1m
| stats avg(speed_fpm) as avg_speed, avg(motor_amps) as avg_amps, max(jam) as jam_flag by line_id, belt_id, _time
| where avg_speed < 10 OR jam_flag=1 OR avg_amps>25
| table _time, line_id, belt_id, avg_speed, avg_amps, jam_flag
```

Understanding this SPL

**Conveyor Belt Speed and Jam Detection** — Belt slowdowns and jams starve downstream stations and can damage product; fast detection limits cascade stops across the line.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="conveyor:sensor"` (line_id, belt_id, speed_fpm, motor_amps, jam_sensor). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Edge Hub. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: conveyor:sensor. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="conveyor:sensor". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **jam** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by line_id, belt_id, _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where avg_speed < 10 OR jam_flag=1 OR avg_amps>25` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Conveyor Belt Speed and Jam Detection**): table _time, line_id, belt_id, avg_speed, avg_amps, jam_flag


Step 3 — Validate
Compare shifts, counts, or yields with the MES or ERP report and the plant historian (for example PI or Proficy) for the same line, order, or batch window. Confirm timestamps and units with the line lead so downtime and quality math line up.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (speed and amps), Status timeline (jam), Table (active issues), Single value (lines stopped).
grandmaExplanation: We watch conveyor speed and jam signals. We help you catch mechanical trouble before the line backs up.
